flashcards.front: Differentiate between a class and an object in Python.
flashcards.back: A class is a blueprint/template that defines attributes and methods for a category of objects. An object (instance) is a concrete entity created from that blueprint, occupying memory and having its own state. Multiple objects can be instantiated from a single class.

flashcards.front: What is encapsulation in Python? How is it implemented?
flashcards.back: Encapsulation bundles data (attributes) and methods that operate on the data into a single unit (class), restricting direct access to some components. Python implements it using naming conventions: underscore (_protected) and double underscore (__private) for name mangling to achieve data hiding.

flashcards.front: Explain abstraction in Python with a practical example.
flashcards.back: Abstraction hides complex implementation details and shows only essential features of an object. In Python, abstract base classes (ABC) with abstract methods enforce subclasses to implement specific methods. Example: A 'Payment' class with abstract method 'process_payment()' without specifying how different payment modes work.

flashcards.front: Demonstrate polymorphism in Python with code example.
flashcards.back: Polymorphism allows objects of different classes to be treated uniformly through a common interface. Python achieves it via duck typing and method overriding. Example: A function that calls len() works on strings, lists, tuples, and custom objects implementing __len__(). Also, child classes overriding parent methods demonstrates polymorphism.

flashcards.front: Distinguish between instance methods, class methods, and static methods in Python.
flashcards.back: Instance methods access/modify object state via 'self' parameter. Class methods access/modify class state via 'cls' parameter, declared with @classmethod decorator. Static methods don't access class or instance data, declared with @staticmethod decorator—they're essentially regular functions grouped by logic.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of __init__ and __str__ methods in Python classes?
flashcards.back: __init__(self) is the constructor that initializes object attributes when an instance is created—it sets up the initial state. __str__(self) returns a human-readable string representation of an object, used by print() and str() functions; unlike __repr__(), it's meant for end-users.

flashcards.front: Explain inheritance and list its types in Python.
flashcards.back: Inheritance allows a child class to acquire properties and methods of a parent class, promoting code reusability. Types include: Single (one parent → one child), Multiple (multiple parents → one child), Multilevel (grandparent → parent → child), Hierarchical (one parent → multiple children), and Hybrid (combination of types).

flashcards.front: What is method overriding and how does it support polymorphism?
flashcards.back: Method overriding occurs when a child class provides a specific implementation of a method already defined in its parent class. It supports polymorphism by allowing the same method call to behave differently based on the object type—Python's interpreter determines which version to execute at runtime based on the actual object.

flashcards.front: Differentiate between class variables and instance variables in Python.
flashcards.back: Class variables are shared across all instances of a class—they're defined inside the class but outside methods. Instance variables are unique to each object, defined inside __init__ or methods using 'self.variable_name'. Modifying a class variable affects all instances unless an instance creates its own copy (shadowing).

flashcards.front: What is composition in OOP and how does it differ from inheritance?
flashcards.back: Composition ('has-a' relationship) creates classes by combining existing classes as components, promoting loose coupling. Inheritance ('is-a' relationship) creates specialized classes from general ones, promoting tight coupling. Composition is preferred over inheritance for flexibility—changes to component classes don't break the containing class, and runtime behavior can be modified.
